title: 3 People Have Gotten Cancer-Detecting Implants in Their Brains
gist: Coherence Neuro, a San Francisco startup with Neuralink ties, has completed first-in-human testing of a brain implant designed to detect and electrically stimulate glioblastoma tumors, with a full permanent-implant trial planned for 2027. The device targets glioblastoma, which kills most patients within 15-18 months, by delivering continuous electrical stimulation—a mechanism validated by Stanford research showing seizure drugs can slow high-grade glioma growth. Competitor Novocure already has a wearable approval for the same indication.

insights.claim: Coherence Neuro placed a coin-sized brain-computer interface in three patients' brains for ~30 minutes during tumor-removal surgery at the Royal Melbourne Hospital, marking the first human safety test of a BCI designed to detect and treat cancer.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: First-in-human milestone de-risks the technology and opens a path to permanent implant trials; watch for IND filing in the US.
insights.quote: Coherence Neuro says it temporarily placed its coin-sized implant in the brains of three people undergoing surgery to have brain tumors removed at the Royal Melbourne Hospital in Australia.

insights.claim: Coherence Neuro plans to begin a trial next year with glioblastoma patients receiving permanent implants, representing a move from safety check to therapeutic efficacy testing.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: If the trial initiates on schedule, clinical readouts could emerge 2028-2029, creating a potential M&A or partnership catalyst for neurotech acquirers.
insights.quote: The company plans to begin a trial next year in glioblastoma patients in which they will have the device permanently implanted.

insights.claim: Glioblastoma patients have a median survival of 15-18 months post-diagnosis and a five-year survival rate below 10%, creating a large unmet need with few competing therapies beyond surgery, chemotherapy, and Novocure's Optune.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: The severity of the unmet need justifies premium valuations for any clinically validated approach; Novocure's Optune approval history sets a regulatory precedent.
insights.quote: Most patients live for just 15 to 18 months after being diagnosed, with a five-year survival rate of less than 10 percent.

insights.claim: Stanford University research published in 2019 showed that high-grade gliomas form synapses with healthy neurons to drive their own growth, and that seizure drugs given to mice interrupted these electrical signals and slowed tumor progression—the mechanistic basis for Coherence's approach.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: Pre-clinical mechanistic validation from a top institution strengthens the scientific credibility of the Coherence approach and reduces early investor uncertainty about the biology.
insights.quote: researchers at Stanford University found that a group of aggressive brain tumors called high-grade gliomas drive their own growth by forming synapses with healthy neurons

insights.claim: Novocure's Optune wearable device, approved in 2011 for glioblastoma and expanded in 2026 to pancreatic cancer, can extend survival by several months but requires head-shaving and constant backpack battery carriage—Coherence targets this convenience gap with a permanent implant.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: Novocure (NVCR) faces competitive disruption if implantable BCI approaches demonstrate comparable or superior efficacy with better adherence; monitor Coherence's trial enrollment.
insights.quote: The Optune device can improve survival by several months if it's worn for most of the day, but people have to shave their heads to use it and carry a battery around in a backpack or on a hip belt.

insights.claim: Neuralink's head neurosurgeon Matthew MacDougall is both an adviser and investor in Coherence Neuro, and Neuralink trial investigator Rory Murphy is expected to participate in future Coherence trials—indicating the broader Neuralink network is incubating cancer-treatment BCI spinouts.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: Neuralink's surgical and scientific network provides Coherence with clinical credibility and talent access; watch for future Neuralink-adjacent companies targeting disease indications beyond paralysis.
insights.quote: Matthew MacDougall, Neuralink's head neurosurgeon, is an adviser and investor in Coherence.

themes.theme: Continuous remote monitoring as new care model

insights.claim: Current glioblastoma standard-of-care relies on MRI scans every 2-3 months to monitor tumor growth, but Coherence's device would enable continuous monitoring and remote therapy adjustment, potentially enabling earlier surgical intervention before MRI-detectable recurrence.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: Continuous monitoring paradigm creates a recurring software/data revenue stream beyond device implant revenue; analog to CGM in diabetes (Dexcom model) applied to neuro-oncology.
insights.quote: by detecting rapid tumor growth, the device could signal to doctors when to intervene with surgery before an MRI could

insights.claim: Coherence's connected app will allow clinicians to remotely fine-tune electrical stimulation therapy or let the device adjust automatically, introducing closed-loop autonomous therapy into cancer treatment.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: Closed-loop autonomous therapy is a high-margin differentiator; if clinically validated, this feature set commands premium reimbursement and creates high switching costs.
insights.quote: Doctors can fine-tune the therapy remotely or let the device do so automatically.
